coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots inhibit T cell-mediated neuroinflammation in rats
coredata.dc:creator: Tosic, Jelena
Stanojevic, Zeljka
Vidicevic, Sasenka
Isakovic, Aleksandra
Ciric, Darko
Martinovic, Tamara
Kravic-Stevovic, Tamara
Bumbasirevic, Vladimir
Paunovic, Verica
Jovanovic, Svetlana
Todorovic-Markovic, Biljana
Markovic, Zoran
Danko, Martin
Micusik, Matej
Spitalsky, Zdenko
Trajkovic, Vladimir
coredata.dc:description: We investigated the therapeutic capacity of nano-sized graphene sheets, called graphene quantum dots (GQD), in experimental autoimmune encephalomyelitis (EAE), an animal model of immune-mediated central nervous system (CNS) damage. Intraperitoneally administered GQD (10 mg/kg/day) accumulated in the lymph node and CNS cells of Dark Agouti rats in which EAE was induced by immunization with spinal cord homogenate in complete Freund's adjuvant. GQD significantly reduced clinical signs of EAE when applied throughout the course of the disease (day 0–32), while the protection was less pronounced if the treatment was limited to the induction (day 0–7 post-immunization) or effector (from day 8 onwards) phase of the disease. GQD treatment diminished immune infiltration, demyelination, axonal damage, and apoptotic death in the CNS of EAE animals. GQD also reduced the numbers of interferon-γ-expressing T helper (Th)1 cells, as well as the expression of Th1 transcription factor T-bet and proinflammatory cytokines tumor necrosis factor, interleukin-1, and granulocyte-macrophage colony-stimulating factor in the lymph nodes and CNS immune infitrates. The protective effect of GQD in EAE was associated with the activation of p38 and p42/44 mitogen-activated protein kinases (MAPK) and Akt in the lymph nodes and/or CNS. Finally, GQD protected oligodendrocytes and neurons from T cell-mediated damage in the in vitro conditions. Collectively, these data demonstrate the ability of GQD to gain access to both immune and CNS cells during neuroinflammation, and to alleviate immune-mediated CNS damage by modulating MAPK/Akt signaling and encephalitogenic Th1 immune response.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Neuroinflammation
Demyelination
T cells
Interferon-γ

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Histological examination of CNS infiltration and demyelination
TEM analysis of GQD internalization, demyelination, and axonal damage
Preparation of lymph node cells (LNC) and CNS-infiltrated MNC
Flow cytometry analysis of T cell cytokine production
Real-time reverse transcription polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR)
Isolation of primary oligodendrocytes and neurons
In vitro assessment of T cell toxicity to oligodendrocytes and neurons
GQD reduce clinical signs of EAE
GQD reduce immune infiltration, apoptosis, and demyelination in the CNS of EAE rats
GQD inhibit production of IFN-γ and proinflammatory cytokines in EAE
GQD increase MAPK and Akt signaling in EAE
GQD accumulate in immune and CNS cells of EAE rats
GQD protect oligodendrocytes and neurons from T cell-mediated damage in vitro

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Education, Science and Technological Development of the Republic of Serbia
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministarstvo Prosvete, Nauke i Tehnološkog Razvoja

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Ministry of Education, Science and Technological Development of the Republic of Serbia (grant numbers: 41025 and 172003 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic overview of experimental workflow. At day 7, 14, or 21 after immunization the appropriate tissues/cells were collected/isolated for further analysis. Cytokine production/expression was measured by flow cytometry and reverse-transcription polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR). The activation of protein kinases was determined by immunoblotting. The immune infiltration of the CNS was evaluated by hematoxylin/eosin (HE) staining, while demyelination was assessed by Luxol fast blue staining and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). TEM was also used for the analysis of GQD internalization by CNS and lymph node cells. Apoptotic cells in the CNS were detected by terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase dUTP nick end labeling (TUNEL). LNC – lymph node cells; MNC – mononuclear cells.

Characterization of GQD. Top view AFM images (a), TEM images (b), and photoluminescence spectra (c) of GQD produced by electrochemical exfoliation of graphite (left) and thermal decomposition of citric acid (right) are shown.

GQD reduce clinical signs of EAE. (a, b) Rats (n = 8 per group) immunized with spinal cord homogenate were injected intraperitoneally with saline (day 0–32; control) (a, b) or graphite-derived GQD (10 mg/kg/day) throughout the course of disease (day 0–32) (a, b), during the induction phase (day 0–7) (a), or during the effector phase of the disease (day 8–32) (a). (c) Rats (n = 20 per group) immunized with spinal cord homogenate were injected intraperitoneally with saline (control) or graphite-derived GQD (10 mg/kg/day) from the day of symptom appearance until the end of the experiment. (d, e) Rats (n = 8 per group) immunized with spinal cord homogenate were injected intraperitoneally with saline or citric acid-derived GQD (10 mg/kg/day) throughout the course of disease (day 0–32). (a–e) Rats were monitored for clinical signs of EAE (a, c, d) and weight changes (b, e) (*p < 0.05 vs. control).

GQD reduce immune infiltration and apoptosis in the CNS of EAE rats. Lumbosacral spinal cord samples were collected at day 14 from healthy (PBS-injected) and EAE rats treated or not with graphite-derived GQD (10 mg/kg/day) (n = 5 rats per group, cumulative score 6.8 ± 2.0 vs. 2.5 ± 2.7 in control EAE vs. GQD treatment). The immune infiltration was assessed by hematoxylin/eosin staining (a, b), and apoptosis was analyzed by TUNEL staining (c, d). Apoptotic oligodendrocytes in EAE rats were detected by TUNEL/MBP double staining (e). Representative micrographs, with arrows pointing at the inflammatory infiltrates and apoptotic cells, are shown in (a) and (b, e), respectively. The quantitative analysis of MNC infiltration and apoptosis is presented in (c) and (d), respectively (*p < 0.05 vs. healthy control; #p < 0.05 vs. EAE control).

GQD reduce demyelination and axonal damage in EAE. Lumbosacral spinal cord samples were collected at day 14 (a, b, d) or day 21 (c, d) from healthy (PBS-injected) and EAE rats treated or not with graphite-derived GQD (10 mg/kg/day). Demyelination was assessed by Luxol fast blue staining (n = 5 rats per group, cumulative score as in Fig. 4) (a, b) or TEM (n = 3 rats per group; day 14 cumulative score 7.8 ± 2.9 vs. 3.0 ± 2.0; day 21 cumulative score 17.0 ± 6.5 vs. 5.5 ± 5.3 in control EAE vs. GQD treatment) (c, d). Representative micrographs are shown in (a) and (c; low magnification – left panel, high magnification – right panel), with white arrows, black arrow, and white arrowhead pointing at demyelination, axonal damage, and GQD, respectively. The quantitative analysis of demyelination by Luxol fast blue staining and TEM is presented in (b) and (d), respectively (*p < 0.05 vs. healthy control; #p < 0.05 vs. EAE control).

The effects of GQD on cytokine production in EAE. The draining popliteal lymph nodes (a, c) or lumbosacral spinal cord MNC infiltrates (b, d) were collected at day 7 or day 14, respectively, from EAE rats treated or not with graphite-derived GQD (10 mg/kg/day) (n = 5 per group, day 14 cumulative score 8.9 ± 3.5 vs. 3.9 ± 3.2 in control EAE vs. GQD treatment). The expression of IFN-γ, IL-17, and IL-10 in CD3+ T cells from LNC (a) and spinal cord MNC (b) was analyzed by flow cytometry, and the results are presented in the upper right quadrant of the dot plot (*p < 0.05 vs. EAE control). The expression of mRNA for different cytokines and transcription factors in the lymph node tissue (c) and spinal cord MNC (d) was determined by RT-PCR, and the results are presented as fold change relative to values obtained in control EAE animals (*p < 0.05).

The effects of GQD on MAPK and Akt signaling in EAE. The draining popliteal lymph nodes or lumbosacral spinal cord samples were collected at day 7 or day 14, respectively, from EAE rats treated or not with graphite-derived GQD (10 mg/kg/day) (n = 4 per group; day 14 cumulative score 6.9 ± 1.4 vs. 3.3 ± 1.8 in control EAE vs. GQD treatment). Cervical lymph nodes and lumbosacral spinal cord were also collected from healthy PBS-injected controls. The phosphorylation of JNK, p38 MAPK, ERK, and Akt was assessed by immunoblotting (the original blots are presented in Supplemental Fig. 1). The results of the densitometry analysis are shown below the corresponding phospho-protein bands (*p < 0.05 vs. healthy control; #p < 0.05 vs. EAE control).

GQD internalization by immune cells, oligodendrocytes, and neurons. (a–d) Lymph nodes (a) or spinal cord samples (b–d) were collected at day 7 or day 14, respectively, from EAE rats treated with graphite-derived GQD (10 mg/kg/day) (n = 3, day 14 cumulative score as in Fig. 5). Representative TEM images (low magnification – upper panel, high magnification – lower panel) show GQD agglomerates (arrows) in the lymphocyte cytoplasm (a), myelin sheet (b), glial cell cytoplasm (c), and the axoplasm of a nerve fiber (d). (e–g) Healthy cervical LNC, OLN-93 oligodendrocytes, and PC12 neuronal cells were incubated with GQD (100 μg/ml) for 24 h. Representative TEM images (low magnification – upper panel, high magnification – lower panel) show GQD (arrows) in the autolysosome of a lymphocyte (e), in the vesicle and cytoplasm of an OLN-93 cell (f), and in the vesicle and cytoplasm of a PC12 cell (g).

GQD protect oligodendrocytes and neurons from T cell-mediated damage. (a) OLN-93 oligodendrocytes or PC12 neuronal cells were incubated for 1 h with or without graphite-derived GQD (50 μg/ml) in the lower chamber of the cell co-culture system. MNC were then placed in the cell culture insert and stimulated or not with Con A (5 μg/ml). After 48 h, the viability of OLN-93 and PC12 cells was examined by MTT assay. (b) OLN-93 cells, PC12 cells, primary oligodendrocytes, or primary neurons were incubated for 1 h with or without graphite-derived GQD (50 μg/ml). A conditioned medium (CM) from unstimulated or Con A-activated LNC was then added, and cell viability was assessed after 48 h by MTT assay. (c) Healthy cervical LNC were stimulated for 8 h with Con A (5 μg/ml), in the presence or absence of GQD (50 μg/ml). The expression of mRNA for different cytokines and transcription factors was determined by RT-PCR, and the results are shown as fold change relative to Con A treatment. (a–c) The presented results are from a representative of three independent experiments done in triplicates (a, b), or from one experiment performed with LNC from 4 rats (*p < 0.05 vs. untreated cells; #p < 0.05 vs. Con A treatment).

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots inhibit T cell-mediated neuroinflammation in rats

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Medical and Clinical Biochemistry, School of Medicine, University of Belgrade, Pasterova 2, 11000, Belgrade, Serbia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Medical and Clinical Biochemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Histology and Embryology, School of Medicine, University of Belgrade, Visegradska 26, 11000, Belgrade, Serbia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Microbiology and Immunology, School of Medicine, University of Belgrade, Dr Subotica 1, 11000, Belgrade, Serbia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Vinca Institute of Nuclear Sciences, University of Belgrade, P.O. Box 522, 11000, Belgrade, Serbia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Polymer Institute, Slovak Academy of Sciences, Dubravska Cesta 9, 84541, Bratislava, Slovakia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We investigated the therapeutic capacity of nano-sized graphene sheets, called graphene quantum dots (GQD), in experimental autoimmune encephalomyelitis (EAE), an animal model of immune-mediated central nervous system (CNS) damage. Intraperitoneally administered GQD (10 mg/kg/day) accumulated in the lymph node and CNS cells of Dark Agouti rats in which EAE was induced by immunization with spinal cord homogenate in complete Freund's adjuvant. GQD significantly reduced clinical signs of EAE when applied throughout the course of the disease (day 0–32), while the protection was less pronounced if the treatment was limited to the induction (day 0–7 post-immunization) or effector (from day 8 onwards) phase of the disease. GQD treatment diminished immune infiltration, demyelination, axonal damage, and apoptotic death in the CNS of EAE animals. GQD also reduced the numbers of interferon-γ-expressing T helper (Th)1 cells, as well as the expression of Th1 transcription factor T-bet and proinflammatory cytokines tumor necrosis factor, interleukin-1, and granulocyte-macrophage colony-stimulating factor in the lymph nodes and CNS immune infitrates. The protective effect of GQD in EAE was associated with the activation of p38 and p42/44 mitogen-activated protein kinases (MAPK) and Akt in the lymph nodes and/or CNS. Finally, GQD protected oligodendrocytes and neurons from T cell-mediated damage in the in vitro conditions. Collectively, these data demonstrate the ability of GQD to gain access to both immune and CNS cells during neuroinflammation, and to alleviate immune-mediated CNS damage by modulating MAPK/Akt signaling and encephalitogenic Th1 immune response.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQD) are 1-10-layer thick sheets of graphene, a carbon allotrope consisting of a single layer of carbon atoms in a honeycomb structure (Zheng et al., 2015). Their small size (<100 nm), high surface-to-volume ratio, excellent electrical properties, strong tunable photo-luminescence, and low toxicity make them potentially useful in nanomedicine as bioimaging agents and biosensors (Hwang et al., 2016; Schroeder et al., 2016). Moreover, because of the presence of surface reactive groups which allow conjugation with various functional groups and biologically active molecules, GQD are ideal candidates for multifunctional nanoplatforms for drug delivery (Iannazzo et al., 2017; Joshi et al., 2016). Due to their ability to donate or accept electrons in the presence or absence of light, respectively, GQD display pro-oxidant/cytotoxic, or antioxidant/cytoprotective properties (Chong et al., 2016). GQD also induce autophagy, an intracellular process for bulk or selective lysosomal removal of unused, damaged, or excessively accumulated macromolecules and organelles (Markovic et al., 2012). However, while immunomodulatory properties of other carbon nanomaterials, such as fullerenes, carbon nanotubes, graphene/graphene oxide have been extensively documented (Dellinger et al., 2013; Dudek et al., 2016; Orecchioni et al., 2014), only few studies so far examined the effects of GQD on immune functions. Qin et al. reported that GQD induce oxidative stress and proinflammatory response in the THP-1 human monocytic cell line (Qin et al., 2015). On the other hand, we have shown that GQD protect mouse liver from immune-mediated damage (Volarevic et al., 2014) and suppress proinflammatory human T cell responses via induction of tolerogenic dendritic cells (Tomic et al., 2017). However, the influence of GQD on the inflammatory injury of other tissues, including the central nervous system (CNS), has not been assessed so far.

The experimental autoimmune encephalomyelitis (EAE) is an animal model of brain inflammation that shares some clinical and pathological features with human CNS demyelinating diseases, such as multiple sclerosis (MS) and acute disseminated encephalomyelitis (Robinson et al., 2014). The immunization of susceptible animals with spinal cord homogenate or specific myelin antigens activates peripheral autoreactive CD4+ T helper (Th) cells, which migrate to the CNS where they are reactivated by target antigens (Rangachari and Kuchroo, 2013). The main culprits for neuroinflammation in EAE are interferon (IFN)-γ-producing Th1 and interleukin (IL)-17-producing Th17 cells (Legroux and Arbour, 2015; Rostami and Ciric, 2013), which secrete and/or stimulate production of proinflammatory cytokines, including granulocyte-macrophage colony-stimulating factor (GM-CSF), tumor necrosis factor (TNF), and IL-1β (Palle et al., 2017; Pare et al., 2017). This leads to an increase in blood-brain barrier permeability and further inflammatory infiltration of the CNS, causing demyelination, neuronal damage, and eventually flaccid paralysis as the most prominent clinical feature of the disease. Depending on the antigen used and the genetic background, rodents can display a monophasic bout of EAE or chronic/relapsing disease, the latter being more relevant for studying MS (Robinson et al., 2014).

In the present study, we demonstrate for the first time the ability of GQD to attenuate inflammatory CNS damage in the rat model of EAE. Our results indicate that GQD reduce demyelination and clinical signs of EAE by interfering with the peripheral activation and proinflammatory function of CNS-reactive Th1 cells, as well as by directly protecting CNS cells from immune-mediated injury.

For the top-down synthesis of GQD, the electrochemical exfoliation of graphite was performed as previously described, using spectroscopic graphite rods both as anode and cathode (Jovanovic et al., 2015). Alternatively, GQD were synthesized bottom-up by thermal decomposition of citric acid (Amjadi et al., 2016). Briefly, citric acid was heated at 200 °C for 30 min under constant stirring. Afterward, NaOH (0.5 M) was added until the pH reached 7.0, and the sample was dialyzed using a 3.5 kDa dialysis tube membrane. The purified GQD solution was dried in a rotary evaporator. Both graphite- and citric acid-derived GQD were suspended in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) at the concentration of 2 mg/ml (maximum water dispersibility ≈ 10 mg/ml), sterilized by γ-irradiation (10 kGy), and stored at 4 °C. The endotoxin content in GQD suspensions was below the FDA-recommended limit of 0.5 EU/ml (Cooper et al., 2010), as determined by the Limulus amebocyte lysate assay. Surface morphology and size of GQD were examined by transmission electron microscope (TEM; JEOL-JEM 1400, Peabody, MA) operated at 120 kV, and atomic force microscope (AFM; Bruker, Karlsruhe, Germany) equipped with ScanAsyst probes, as previously described (Markovic et al., 2012). The fluorescence spectra were recorded on an RF-5301PC spectrofluorophotometer (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan) in a 1 cm cuvette in the right angle arrangement. Zeta potential was assessed using Zetasizer Nano-ZS (Malvern Instruments, Malvern, UK) equipped with a 4 mW helium/neon laser (λ = 633 nm) and thermo-electric temperature controller. All measurements were done at 25 °C, and the value of zeta potential was averaged from five measurements. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed as previously reported (Tomic et al., 2017), using a K-Alpha XPS system (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA) equipped with a micro-focused, monochromatic Al K-Alpha X-ray source (1486.6 eV).

All animal experiments were approved by the Local Ethics Committee for Animal Experimentation (School of Medicine, University of Belgrade) and carried out in accordance with EU Directive 2010/63/EU for animal experiments and the ARRIVE guidelines (Kilkenny et al., 2010). All efforts were made to minimize animal suffering, reduce the number of animals used, and utilize alternatives to in vivo techniques, if possible. To examine a therapeutic potential of GQD in neuroinflammation, we used the experimental model in which chronic relapsing EAE is induced in Dark Agouti (DA) rats immunized with an emulsion containing homogenized spinal cord and complete Freund's adjuvant (Dahlman et al., 1999). Female 7 week-old DA rats (mean weight ± SD = 96.3 ± 10.8 g) were obtained from the Institute for Medical Research of the Military Medical Academy (Belgrade, Serbia). The rats were housed in the local animal facility (≤3 rats per cage) under conventional conditions with laboratory chow and water ad libitum. To exclude any infections, the animals' health status was monitored according to the Federation of European Laboratory Animal Science Associations (FELASA) guidelines (Nicklas et al., 2002). For EAE induction, 100 μl of the emulsion made by mixing the equal volumes of rat spinal cord homogenate in PBS (50% w/v) and complete Freund's adjuvant (Difco Laboratories, Detroit, MI) were injected under ether anesthesia intradermally into each hind footpad. Healthy controls were injected with 100 μl of PBS. The rats were monitored in a blind fashion for clinical signs of EAE and scored daily using the following scale: 0, no clinical signs; 1, flaccid tail; 2, hind limb paresis; 3, hind limb paralysis; 4, moribund state or death (Badovinac et al., 1998). The following clinical parameters were used: onset of EAE, the first day clinical signs were observed; cumulative EAE score, the sum of all daily clinical scores; maximum EAE score, the highest clinical score observed during disease; and relapse, a sustained (≥2 days) increase of at least one full grade in clinical score after the initial recovery. At the appropriate time-points (day 7, 14, or 21), the rats were sacrificed by cervical dislocation and the tissues (draining lymph nodes and/or spinal cord) were collected for further analysis, as schematically depicted in Fig. 1 .

After immunization, the animals were randomized into control and treatment groups (≤3 rats per cage). The rats in the treatment group were intraperitoneally injected with GQD (10 mg/kg/day) in four different settings: (i) from the day of immunization until the end of experiment, (ii) during the first 7 days, (iii) from day 8 post-immunization until the end of the experiment, (iv) or from the day when first symptoms appeared until the end of the experiment. Control animals were injected intraperitoneally with the appropriate volume of PBS. The dose of GQD was chosen based on a previous study in which no toxicity was observed after intraperitoneal administration of 20 mg/kg every other day for 14 days (Chong et al., 2014).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Histological examination of CNS infiltration and demyelination
ce:section.ce:para: Surgically removed samples of lumbosacral spinal cord were fixed in 4% paraformaldehyde and embedded in paraffin. Serial tissue sections (5 μm thick) were deparaffinized in xylol, dehydrated in increasing alcohol concentrations, and stained with hematoxylin/eosin or Luxol fast blue (both from Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO) to detect mononuclear cell (MNC) infiltration and demyelination, respectively. For the latter, slides were stained with prewarmed 0.5% Luxol fast blue, differentiated in 0.05% lithium carbonate and 70% ethanol, and counterstained with Cresyl violet (all from Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO). Digital images of the stained sections were made on a Leica DM4000B LED microscope equipped with a Digital Camera (Leica DFC295) and LAS 4.4 Software (Leica Microsystems, Wetzlar, Germany). Morphometric analysis of areas with MNC infiltrates was done in Fiji (ImageJ 1.49b; NIH, Bethesda, MD) using ‘analyze measurements’ tool. The spared white matter in Luxol fast blue-stained sections was calculated in Fiji as the total section area minus the non-stained area, and reported as a percentage of total area (de Almeida et al., 2011). The quantification of both CNS infiltrates and sparred white matter was done in five randomly chosen spinal cord sections per rat.

DNA fragmentation (as an apoptotic marker) was detected in the paraformaldehyde-fixed, paraffin-embedded lumbosacral spinal cord sections by terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase dUTP nick end labeling (TUNEL), using In Situ Cell Death Detection Kit (Roche, Mannheim, Germany) as previously described (Popovic et al., 2015). Apoptotic cells, showing red nuclear staining, were counted in five randomly chosen spinal cord sections per rat. Apoptotic oligodendrocytes were detected by TUNEL/myelin basic protein (MBP) double staining as previously described (Biran et al., 2006), using primary mouse anti-MBP (1:200, Invitrogen, Carlsbad, CA; MA5-15922, clone 2H9) and secondary goat anti-mouse IgG-TRITC (1:200, SouthernBiotech, Birmingham, AL; 1031-03) after TUNEL labeling.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TEM analysis of GQD internalization, demyelination, and axonal damage
ce:section.ce:para: Surgically removed specimens of lumbosacral spinal cords and draining popliteal lymph nodes, or trypsinized cells from cell cultures were fixed with 3% glutaraldehyde in cacodylate buffer and postfixed in 1% osmium tetroxide. After dehydration in graded alcohols, cells were embedded in Epoxy medium (Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO). Thin sections were mounted on copper grids (Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO) and stained with uranyl acetate and lead citrate for examination on an electron microscope (Morgagni 268D, FEI, Hillsboro, OR). Quantitative analysis of demyelination and axonal degeneration was performed on 20 randomly selected fields of lumbosacral spinal cord electron microscopic images (20–40 nerve fibers per field, 4400 × magnification) per rat. Demyelination was seen as the absence of myelin sheet around the axon, while axonal degeneration was determined as the accumulation of damaged, electron-dense organelles in the axoplasm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of lymph node cells (LNC) and CNS-infiltrated MNC
ce:section.ce:para: The draining popliteal lymph nodes from immunized rats and cervical lymph nodes from healthy rats were passed through a steel mesh in RPMI-1640 medium (Sigma-Aldrich, St Louis, MO), and the obtained LNC suspension was filtered through a nylon mesh, washed, and resuspended in RPMI-1640 with 2% rat serum (Sigma-Aldrich, St Louis, MO). MNC were isolated from the spinal cords of immunized rats as previously described (Popovic et al., 2015). Briefly, the spinal cords of animals perfused with sterile PBS were dissociated through a wire mesh and centrifuged at 700 g for 2 min. The pellets were then resuspended in 40% Percoll (Sigma-Aldrich, St Louis, MO) and centrifuged on a discontinuous 40%/70% Percoll gradient at 850 g for 25 min. The spinal cord MNC were collected from the 40%/70% interface, washed, and resuspended in RPMI-1640 medium supplemented with 2% rat serum. The purity of MNC was >95%, as determined by May-Grünwald-Giemsa staining (not shown).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Flow cytometry analysis of T cell cytokine production
ce:section.ce:para: For the flow cytometry analysis, LNC and spinal cord-infiltrated MNC were incubated for 4 h with phorbol myristate acetate (100 ng/ml), ionomycin (400 ng/ml), and brefeldin A (5 μM) (all from Sigma-Aldrich, St Louis, MO) in RPMI-1640 medium with 2% rat serum. The cells were stained for the T cell marker CD3 and intracellular content of IFN-γ, IL-17, and IL-10 by using the fixation/permeabilization kit and anti-rat monoclonal antibodies conjugated with fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC-CD3; BD Biosciences, San Jose, CA), AlexaFluor 647 (IFN-γ; BioLegend, San Diego, CA), phycoerythrin (PE-IL-10; BD Biosciences, San Jose, CA), or peridinin chlorophyll protein (PercP)-Cy5.5 (IL-17; eBioscience, San Diego, CA), following the manufacturers’ instructions. The stained cells were analyzed using a FACSAria III flow cytometer (BD Biosciences, San Jose, CA), and the results were analyzed with the FlowJo software (LLC, Ashland, OR).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real-time reverse transcription polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR)
ce:section.ce:para: RNA was isolated from the lymph node tissue or spinal cord-infiltrated MNC, and RT-PCR was performed as previously described (Popovic et al., 2015), using a Realplex Mastercycler (Eppendorf, Hamburg, Germany), TaqMan Master Mix, and the following TaqMan primers and probes (all from Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA): IFN-γ (Rn00594078_m1), Tbx21/T-bet (Rn01461633_ m1), IL-17A (Rn01757168_m1), RORγt (Rn01533717_g1), IL-10 (Rn 00563409_m1), TNF (Rn999990 17_m1), IL-1β (Rn00580432_m1), GM-CSF (Rn01456850_m1), and 18s rRNA (Hs99999901_s1) as a house-keeping gene. The threshold cycle (Ct) values of 18s rRNA were subtracted from the Ct values of target genes to obtain ΔCt, and the relative gene expression was determined as 2−ΔCt.

Protein extraction from popliteal/cervical lymph nodes or spinal cord-infiltrated MNC and immunoblotting were performed as previously described (Popovic et al., 2015). Because popliteal lymph nodes from non-immunized animals were too small for analysis, cervical lymph nodes from PBS-injected rats were used as a healthy control lymphoid tissue. The rabbit antibodies against p38 mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK), phospho-p38 MAPK (Thr180/Tyr182), extracellular signal-regulated kinase (ERK), phospho-ERK (Thr202/Tyr204), c-Jun-N-terminal kinase (JNK), phospho-JNK (Thr183/Tyr185), Akt, phospho-Akt (Ser473), and actin (all from Cell Signaling Technology, Cambridge, MA) were used as primary antibodies, and peroxidase-conjugated goat anti-rabbit IgG (Southern Biotech, Birmingham, AL) was used as a secondary antibody. The specific protein bands were visualized by enhanced chemiluminescence (GE Healthcare, Pollards Wood, UK) using ChemiDoc imaging system and ImageLab software (Bio-Rad, Hercules, CA). The results are expressed relative to the intensity of total protein signals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Isolation of primary oligodendrocytes and neurons
ce:section.ce:para: Primary spinal cord/brain stem oligodendrocytes and cerebellar neurons were isolated from DA rats by mechanical and enzymatic digestion as previously described (Oberdoerster, 2001; Vick et al., 1990). Oligodendrocytes were incubated in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's medium (DMEM)/Ham's F-12 mixture (1:1) containing 15 mM HEPES, 3 g/l glucose, 5 g/l Na2HCO3, 1.6 mM glutamate, 20% inactivated horse serum, 5 μg/ml insulin, 30 nM sodium selenite, 50 μg/ml transferrin, 20 μg/ml hydrocortisone, 1 mg/ml bovine serum albumin, and 1% penicillin/streptomycin (all from Sigma-Aldrich, St Louis, MO). Primary neurons were incubated in neurobasal medium with B27 supplement (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA), 0.5 mM glutamine, and 1% penicillin/streptomycin (Sigma- Aldrich, St Louis, MO). Before used for experiments, primary oligodendrocytes and neurons were incubated for 7 days in poly-l-lysine-coated 96-well plates (1 × 104 cells/well) at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere with 5% CO2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro assessment of T cell toxicity to oligodendrocytes and neurons
ce:section.ce:para: Rat oligodendrocyte cell line OLN-93 (Richter-Landsberg and Heinrich, 1996) was a kind gift from Marcus Kipp (Institute of Neuroanatomy, Faculty of Medicine, Aachen, Germany), and was maintained in DMEM supplemented with 5% fetal calf serum and 1% penicillin/streptomycin (all from Sigma- Aldrich, St Louis, MO). Rat pheochromocytoma PC12 cell line was obtained from the European Collection of Cell Cultures (ECACC, Salisbury, UK) and grown in DMEM with high glucose (4.5 g/l) supplemented with 10% horse serum, 1% glutamine, 1% penicillin/streptomycin, (all from Sigma-Aldrich, St Louis, MO), and 5% FetalClone III serum (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA). A single cell suspension of LNC was obtained from the cervical lymph nodes of healthy animals by passing through a steel mesh, and subsequent filtering through a nylon mesh. For the cell co-cultivation experiments, OLN-93 cells (1.5 × 105 cells/well) or PC12 cells (3.5 × 105 cells/well) were incubated in 12-well plates, while LNC (1 × 106) were seeded in permeable inserts with 0.4 μM pores (Corning Life Sciences, Tewksbury, MA), and stimulated with the T cell mitogen Concanavalin A (Con A; Sigma-Aldrich, St Louis, MO). Alternatively, OLN-93 oligodendrocytes (1.5 × 105 cells/well) or PC12 neurons (3.5 × 105 cells/well) were incubated in 12-well plates with the 24 h conditioned medium (25%) from untreated or Con A-stimulated normal LNC. Primary oligodendrocytes and neurons were incubated in 96-well plates (1 × 104 cells/well) with the 24 h conditioned medium (25%) from untreated or Con A-stimulated normal LNC. Cells were incubated in the appropriate neuronal or oligodendrocyte cell culture medium (37 °C, 5% CO2) for 48 h prior to viability assessment.

The viability of OLN-93/PC12 cells and primary oligodendrocytes/neurons was determined by colorimetric assessment (570 nm) of mitochondrial dehydrogenase-mediated conversion of 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) to purple formazan, as previously described (Raicevic et al., 2005). The results are presented as % viability of the untreated control, which was set to 100%.

The data are presented as mean ± SD values, and the differences between two or more independent groups were analyzed using the t-test, one-way ANOVA followed by Tukey's test, or Chi-square test where appropriate. A p value of <0.05 was considered significant.

GQD were prepared by both top-down and bottom-up approaches, using electrochemical exfoliation of graphite and thermal decomposition of citric acid, respectively (Amjadi et al., 2016; Jovanovic et al., 2015). According to AFM analysis, the lateral size of graphite- and citric acid-derived GQD was 23.6 ± 7.0 nm (mean ± SD, n = 100) and 13.5 ± 6.0 nm, respectively, with the average height of approx. 2 nm, corresponding to two graphene layers (Fig. 2 a). TEM analysis confirmed the oval shape and approx. 20 nm diameter of GQD (Fig. 2b). In accordance with previous findings (Jovanovic et al., 2015), the photoluminescence spectra showed that GQD had the highest photoluminescence intensity in the blue region at 330 nm excitation, shifting to higher wavelengths with increasing excitation wavelength (Fig. 2c). The XPS analysis revealed a higher sp2/sp3 bond ratio in graphite-compared to citric acid-derived GQD (58.8/27.7 at% vs. 34.6/33.5 at%), suggesting higher crystallinity and more ordered structure of the former (Jiang et al., 2015). On the other hand, citric acid-derived GQD displayed a higher degree of oxygen functionalization at the surface, as indicated by the increased presence of C-O, C=O, and O-C=O bonds (19.6, 6.3, and 5.8 at%, respectively) compared to graphite-derived GQD (8.4, 5.1, and 0.0 at%). The zeta potential value, as a measure of the particle surface charge, was −14.0 ± 2.1 mV and −26.3 ± 3.6 mV for graphite- and citric acid-derived GQD, respectively, indicating that both suspensions were electrostatically stabilized. The lower zeta potential value of citric acid-derived GQD might be due to the increase of negative surface charge resulting from deprotonation of carboxyl functional groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQD reduce clinical signs of EAE
ce:section.ce:para: We first evaluated the ability of GQD to modulate clinical symptoms of EAE induced in rats by immunization with spinal cord homogenate in complete Freund's adjuvant (Badovinac et al., 1998). Graphite-derived GQD, administered from the day of immunization until the end of the experiment (day 0–32), significantly reduced the daily mean clinical score at several time-points (Fig. 3 a). The protective action of GQD in EAE was confirmed by the delayed onset of the disease (10.8 ± 0.84 vs. 9.00 ± 1.10 days in GQD-treated vs. control EAE rats; p < 0.05), as well as by lower cumulative (8.83 ± 7.03 vs. 26.0 ± 12.6; p < 0.05) and maximum score (0.75 ± 0.69 vs. 2.42 ± 0.75; p < 0.05). As a consequence of inflammation-induced anorexia, animals with EAE display a reduction in body weight, which reflects disease severity (Pollak et al., 2000). Accordingly, we observed a transient decrease in body mass of rats at the peak of EAE, which was prevented by treatment with GQD (Fig. 3b). When GQD were applied only during the induction (day 0–7) or effector phase (from day 8 onwards) of the disease, the protective effect was clearly less prominent and significant only at some time-points (Fig. 3a). Both the cumulative score (15.5 ± 3.0) and maximum score (1.58 ± 0.75) were significantly lower in 0–7-day treatment group compared to control (p < 0.05). On the other hand, neither cumulative (19.1 ± 6.15) nor maximum score (2.17 ± 0.90) was significantly different from the control values (p > 0.05) in 8–32-day treatment group. Another important therapeutic parameter in EAE is the ability to reduce the incidence of disease relapses. Because of the relatively low incidence of relapses in our model, we performed another experiment with larger groups (n = 20) to assess the effect of therapeutic GQD administration on the relapse frequency in EAE. In this experiment, the group in which each animal started receiving GQD on the day of symptom appearance displayed a significantly lower relapse frequency (5%, 1 of 20 rats) compared to control EAE group (35%, 7 of 20 rats; p < 0.05), as well as significantly milder clinical score at the late stage of the disease (Fig. 3c). Therefore, although therapeutic application of GQD was not as effective as the preventive treatment in mitigating the first EAE attack, it effectively prevented the disease relapse. Collectively, these data indicate that GQD are able to interfere with both the activation of immune cells at the periphery and their reactivation/effector functions in the CNS in the early and late phases of EAE, respectively. One might argue that the latter effect, which was apparently less prominent than the former, is more important for the potential therapeutic application of GQD in neuroinflammation. However, the interference of GQD with the immune cell activation might also be therapeutically relevant, having in mind that disease relapse in multiple sclerosis patients is associated with the activation of apparently dormant T lymphocytes in the peripheral blood (Fransson et al., 2009). The treatment with citric acid-derived GQD displayed similar, although somewhat less pronounced effects on EAE (Fig. 3d), as confirmed by lower cumulative score (19.0 ± 5.8 vs. 28.0 ± 7.1 in GQD-treated vs. control EAE rats; p < 0.05) and the improvement in weight gain (Fig. 3e). The maximum score was also lower (1.6 ± 1.0 vs. 2.6 ± 0.8; p > 0.05), and the onset was slightly delayed (11.3 ± 3.3 vs. 9.7 ± 1.3 days; p > 0.05), but the differences did not reach statistical significance. It remains to be explored whether the different efficacy of the two GQD preparations might be related to the differences in their physicochemical properties (e.g. size and/or surface functionalization). For further experiments, we have chosen graphite-derived GQD because of their better protective effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQD reduce immune infiltration, apoptosis, and demyelination in the CNS of EAE rats
ce:section.ce:para: During EAE, CNS-infiltrating T cells and macrophages cause oligodendrocyte and neuronal apoptotic death and demyelination by direct contact, as well as by producing or stimulating the microglial production of cytotoxic mediators such as TNF and reactive oxygen/nitrogen species (Das et al., 2008; Herz et al., 2010). Therefore, we assessed the influence of GQD administration on the size of immune cell infiltrates, as well as on CNS cell death and demyelination in the diseased animals. The microscopic analysis of hematoxylin/eosin-stained lumbosacral spinal cord sections, performed at the peak of disease (day 14), revealed an extensive subpial/perivascular MNC infiltration in animals with EAE (Fig. 4 a, middle). On the other hand, MNC infiltrates were clearly more discrete in rats treated with GQD (Fig. 4a, bottom), and completely absent in untreated rats (Fig. 4a, top). Accordingly, the morphometric analysis demonstrated that the spinal cord area containing infiltrated MNC was reduced in GQD-treated animals, compared to EAE controls (Fig. 4b). The analysis of DNA fragmentation by TUNEL assay demonstrated an increase in the proportion of apoptotic cells in the CNS of EAE compared to healthy animals, while the number of apoptotic cells was significantly reduced in EAE rats that received GQD (Fig. 4c and d). As for the type of apoptotic cells, with the exception of rare subpial TUNEL+ nuclei probably belonging to the infiltrated leukocytes, TUNEL+ cells were usually found scattered within the white matter of EAE spinal cords. TUNEL/MBP double staining indicated that these cells were mostly oligodendrocytes, as their TUNEL+ nuclei were embedded among myelinated fibers in close proximity to the vacuolated space enclosed by MBP-positive structures (Fig. 4e). Luxol fast blue staining of spinal cord sections confirmed that demyelination present in EAE tissue samples was also markedly diminished in GQD-treated EAE animals (Fig. 5 a and b). The effects of GQD treatment on the ultrastructural morphology of axons and myelin sheath were assessed by TEM (Fig. 5c and d). Compared to myelinated axons with well preserved neurofilaments and microtubules in the axoplasm of healthy animals (Fig. 5c, top), TEM analysis confirmed the increased presence of unmyelinated axons in the white matter of EAE spinal cords (Fig. 5c, middle). Additionally, some of the axons in EAE rats appeared injured, containing disorganized axoplasm and electron-dense organelles, as well as mitochondria that were swollen and deformed, often with condensed matrix and mitochondrial cristae that were ill-defined or even completely lacking. This finding is consistent with the model in which the myelin loss in EAE could be at least in part secondary to axonal damage (Das et al., 2008). EAE animals treated with GQD had significantly less unmyelinated and damaged axons with much milder mitochondrial injury (Fig. 5c, bottom), as confirmed by the quantitative analysis of demyelination and axonal degeneration (Fig. 5d). Together, these data demonstrate that GQD treatment reduced immune infiltration and the subsequent damage of the CNS during EAE. Accordingly, our previous findings in mice demonstrated the ability of GQD to prevent inflammatory infiltration and apoptotic liver damage during Con A-induced hepatitis (Volarevic et al., 2014). However, as it has been shown that apoptosis of both CNS resident cells and infiltrating lymphocytes contributes to CNS injury and resolution of inflammation in EAE, respectively (Reichardt and Luhder, 2012), a more detailed analysis of apoptotic cell types is required to completely understand the role of GQD anti-apoptotic action in this disease.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQD inhibit production of IFN-γ and proinflammatory cytokines in EAE
ce:section.ce:para: The induction and effector phases of EAE correspond respectively to the activation/expansion of encephalitogenic T cells at the periphery and their re-activation by autoantigens in the CNS (Rangachari and Kuchroo, 2013; Robinson et al., 2014). Thus, we next analyzed the ability of GQD to affect T cell cytokine production in the draining popliteal lymph nodes 7 days after immunization (induction phase) and in the CNS-infiltrated MNC at the peak of the disease (effector phase). To that aim, we measured the expression of proinflammatory cytokines TNF, IL-1, and GM-CSF, anti-inflammatory/immunosuppressive cytokine IL-10, Th1 cytokine IFN-γ, Th17 cytokine IL-17, as well as signature transcription factors of Th1 (T-bet) and Th17 (RORγt) cells, at the protein and/or mRNA levels. The flow cytometry analysis of intracellular cytokines demonstrated a significant decrease in numbers of IFN-γ-, but not IL-17- or IL-10-expressing CD3+ T cells, both in the LNC suspensions (Fig. 6 a) and CNS infiltrates (Fig. 6b) of GQD-treated EAE rats compared to EAE controls. Accordingly, the expression of mRNA encoding IFN-γ and the Th1 transcription factor T-bet were significantly lower in the lymph node tissue (Fig. 6c) and CNS mononuclear infiltrates (Fig. 6d) of EAE rats treated with GQD. Moreover, GQD treatment also reduced the mRNA levels of proinflammatory cytokines TNF, IL-1, and GM-CSF in the CNS-infiltrating MNC, while in the lymph nodes only the difference in TNF reached statistical significance (Fig. 6c and d). GQD administration did not affect the mRNA levels of IL-17, Th17 transcription factor RORγt, and anti-inflammatory IL-10 (Fig. 6c and d). We have previously observed a similar selective downregulation of IFN-γ-producing Th1 and proinflammatory responses in mice with Con A-induced liver injury (Volarevic et al., 2014). It should be noted, however, that GQD prepared by hydrothermal oxidation of graphite induced p38 MAPK/nuclear factor-κB-dependent production of proinflammatory cytokines TNF and IL-8 in THP-1 monocytic leukemia cell line in vitro (Qin et al., 2015). The apparent discrepancy could be related to the different methods used for GQD preparation, the differences between the in vitro and in vivo responses, and/or the differences in responses of the transformed vs. primary cells. While this remains to be explored in more detail, our present findings indicate that the protective effects of GQD in neuroinflammatory damage during EAE mainly result from the interference with both peripheral and CNS activation/function of IFN-γ-secreting encephalitogenic Th1 cells. Having in mind that Th1 and Th17 cells induce EAE through a synergistic cooperation of different pathogenic mechanisms (Domingues et al., 2010), it is plausible that interfering with one Th lineage (in this case Th1) could be sufficient to mitigate the disease by reducing the synergistic pathogenic interaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQD increase MAPK and Akt signaling in EAE
ce:section.ce:para: MAPK and Akt are interacting signaling proteins that regulate both innate and adaptive immunity (Jeffrey et al., 2007; Wang and Liu, 2007; Zhang et al., 2013), as well as neuroinflammation and subsequent CNS damage (Bachstetter and Van Eldik, 2010; Pollak et al., 2005). Therefore, we analyzed the effects of GQD on the activation of Akt and the principal MAPK family members p38 MAPK, ERK, and JNK in the lymph nodes and spinal cord tissue of EAE rats. The phosphorylation of JNK and Akt in the lymph nodes was not significantly modulated in either control or GQD-treated EAE animals (Fig. 7 ). On the other hand, the activation of ERK and p38 MAPK was markedly reduced in the lymph nodes of EAE compared to non-immunized rats and partly restored by GQD treatment (Fig. 7). In the spinal cord, there were no changes in the phosphorylation of JNK, while that of ERK, p38 MAPK, and Akt was significantly reduced in EAE compared to non-immunized rats (Fig. 7). The treatment with GQD increased the phosphorylation of ERK, p38 MAPK, and Akt in the spinal cord of EAE animals (Fig. 7). Therefore, the protective effect of GQD in EAE was associated with an increase in the activation of ERK/p38 MAPK and Akt in the immune and/or CNS cells. Accordingly, GQD were previously reported to induce p38 MAPK activation in human T cell and/or macrophage cell lines (Qin et al., 2015; Volarevic et al., 2014). Since ERK1-deficient mice display an increased IFN-γ production and more severe EAE (Agrawal et al., 2006), the protective action of GQD in EAE could be at least partly explained by the increase in ERK activation and subsequent decrease in differentiation/activation of IFN-γ-producing encephalitogenic Th1 cells. This is indeed consistent with the reduced numbers of CD3+IFN-γ+ cells, and the reduced expression of IFN-γ and its transcription factor T-bet in the lymph nodes (Fig. 6a, c) and mononuclear CNS infiltrates (Fig. 6b, d) in EAE rats treated with GQD. On the other hand, it has been shown that p38 MAPK is required for optimal differentiation of encephalitogenic Th17 cells, rather than being involved in regulation of Th1 development (Noubade et al., 2011). In that context, our finding that p38 MAPK activation was reduced in the lymph nodes of EAE rats might seem somewhat surprising, possibly resulting from p38 MAPK modulation in cells other than T lymphocytes. Also, despite the GQD-mediated increase in p38 MAPK activation in the immune cells during EAE, we did not observe any effects of GQD on IL-17 mRNA or protein expression, indicating that p38-dependent Th17 modulation was not involved in GQD effects in EAE. Since both ERK and Akt have been shown to protect neurons and oligodendrocytes during EAE (Diem et al., 2003; Kumar et al., 2013; Rajendran et al., 2017), it is plausible that the increase in ERK and Akt activity in the CNS was involved in GQD-mediated protection from CNS apoptosis and demyelination in the present study. The effect on p38 MAPK activation was clearly less pronounced, so its contribution to the neuroprotective action of GQD in EAE was presumably minor compared to that of ERK and Akt. It remains on further studies to explore if GQD-mediated activation of MAPK and Akt signaling might be related to their ability to modulate ROS production and autophagy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQD accumulate in immune and CNS cells of EAE rats
ce:section.ce:para: To get an additional insight into the mechanism of GQD-mediated EAE attenuation, we assessed the ability of GQD to enter immune cells as the major culprits, and CNS cells as the major targets of neuroinflammation. TEM analysis revealed that GQD were readily detectable in the lymphocytes of the draining lymph nodes after 7 days (Fig. 8 a), as well as in the white matter of the spinal cord, both in the glial cells and axons of EAE rats after 14 days of treatment (Fig. 8b–d). It should be noted, however, that no CNS accumulation of GQD was observed in non-immunized animals (data not shown). As the integrity of the blood-brain barrier is disrupted in EAE (Bennett et al., 2010), it appears that GQD could gain access to CNS through damaged, but not intact blood-brain barrier. This nevertheless poses the question of the fate of GQD accumulated in the CNS and other tissues, which might be a limiting factor for their therapeutic application. Recent findings indicate that graphene can be degraded in vivo in the liver, spleen, and lung macrophages (Girish et al., 2013), probably by myeloperoxidase (Kurapati et al., 2015), a lysosomal enzyme also present in microglia, and to a lower extent in neurons (Campuzano et al., 2011; Green et al., 2004). Thus, one could expect that GQD will be at least partly degraded in, and eliminated from the CNS and other tissues. We also analyzed the internalization of GQD by the lymph node lymphocytes (Fig. 8e), oligodendrocyte cell line OLN-93 (Fig. 8f), and neuronal cell line PC12 (Fig. 8g). All three cell types efficiently internalized GQD, which could be found in vesicles as well as cytoplasm (Fig. 8e–g). These data demonstrate that GQD can gain access to both immune and CNS cells, which could be related to their ability to modulate the course of EAE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQD protect oligodendrocytes and neurons from T cell-mediated damage in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: To examine if GQD accumulation in the CNS cells might protect them from immune-mediated injury, we assessed the influence of GQD on the survival of neurons and oligodendrocytes co-cultured with LNC activated with the T cell mitogen Con A. The cells of the rat oligodendrocyte cell line OLN-93 or rat neuronal cell line PC12 were incubated with GQD in the lower chamber of the co-culture system, while Con A-activated LNC were placed in the cell culture insert. In agreement with our previous findings (Popovic et al., 2015), Con A-stimulated LNC significantly reduced the viability of PC12 and OLN-93 cells in this setting, as confirmed by the reduced mitochondrial dehydrogenase activity (Fig. 9 a). The co-incubation of PC12 or OLN-93 cells with GQD resulted in a significant protection from the cytotoxicity of Con A-stimulated LNC (Fig. 9a). GQD also protected OLN-93 and PC12, as well as primary oligodendrocytes and neurons cells from the toxicity of the conditioned medium obtained from Con A-stimulated LNC (Fig. 9b), suggesting a direct pro-survival effect on CNS cells. Finally, the expression of mRNA encoding the Th1 mediators IFN-γ and T-bet, as well as proinflammatory cytokines TNF, IL-1, and GM-CSF, was reduced in Con A-stimulated LNC treated with GQD (Fig. 9c), thus confirming their anti-Th1 and anti-inflammatory action. GQD did not alter the expression of anti-inflammatory IL-10 (Fig. 9c). In contrast to the in vivo results (Fig. 6c), GQD reduced the mRNA levels of Th17 mediators IL-17 and RORγt in Con A-treated LNC, although the difference in IL-17 did not reach statistical significance (p = 0.061) (Fig. 9c). This indicates that Th17 cells might be more sensitive to GQD inhibition in the in vitro compared to in vivo conditions, possibly due to the higher dose of nanoparticles in the cell culture. It remains to be resolved if GQD affect Th1 cell development directly or by reducing the expression of Th1-inducing cytokines (e.g. IL-12) in antigen-presenting macrophages and/or dendritic cells. In accordance with the latter possibility, we have recently described the ability of GQD to suppress Th1-inducing capacity of human dendritic cells (Tomic et al., 2017). On the other hand, our data cannot exclude the possibility that GQD might additionally protect the CNS of EAE animals by reducing the extravasation of Th1 and/or other immune cells through the blood-brain barrier. Nevertheless, our data indicate that both the interference with Th1 cell activation/function and direct protection of CNS cells contribute to EAE attenuation by GQD.

The present study demonstrates the ability of GQD to gain access to both immune and CNS cells during neuroinflammation in vivo, and to alleviate immune-mediated CNS damage in a rat model of EAE. The observed protection was associated with the activation of MAPK/Akt signaling, possibly resulting in the suppression of the encephalitogenic Th1 immune response and CNS inflammation, as well as in the direct protection of oligodendrocytes and neurons from the inflammatory damage. These data support further investigation of GQD as potential candidates for the therapy of T cell-mediated neuroinflammation.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Ministry of Education, Science and Technological Development of the Republic of Serbia (grant numbers: 41025 and 172003).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Fig 1 Supplementary Fig 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.neuropharm.2018.11.030.
